From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Not to be confused with [NHS Improvement](/wiki/NHS_Improvement "NHS Improvement").

British government health agency

Office for Health Improvement and Disparities

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Agency overview | |
| Preceding agency | * [Public Health England](/wiki/Public_Health_England "Public Health England") |
| Jurisdiction | [England](/wiki/England "England") |
| Ministers responsible | * [Ashley Dalton](/wiki/Ashley_Dalton "Ashley Dalton") * Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Public Health and Prevention |
| Agency executives | * [Professor Chris Whitty](/wiki/Chris_Whitty "Chris Whitty"), [Chief Medical Officer for England](/wiki/Chief_Medical_Officer_for_England "Chief Medical Officer for England") * Dr Jeanelle de Gruchy, [Deputy Chief Medical Officer for England](/wiki/Chief_Medical_Officers_(United_Kingdom) "Chief Medical Officers (United Kingdom)") * Jonathan Marron, Director General |
| Parent agency | [Department of Health and Social Care](/wiki/Department_of_Health_and_Social_Care "Department of Health and Social Care") |
| Website | <https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/office-for-health-improvement-and-disparities> |

The **Office for Health Improvement and Disparities (OHID)** is a government unit within the [Department of Health and Social Care](/wiki/Department_of_Health_and_Social_Care "Department of Health and Social Care")[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) that leads national efforts to improve public health policy across England.

The body is a successor organisation to [Public Health England](/wiki/Public_Health_England "Public Health England"), and is responsible for health improvement and public health functions along with [NHS England](/wiki/NHS_England "NHS England"), as outlined in correspondence on the location of Public Health England functions from 1 October 2021.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Office focuses on reducing the burdens of preventable illness and disease, and of health inequalities, on society and the healthcare system.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It explores how incentives and rewards can encourage healthier behaviour, with an explicit focus on the work of the [Health Promotion Board](/wiki/Health_Promotion_Board "Health Promotion Board") in Singapore.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The OHID became fully operational on 1 October 2021.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
